rulings.Case Title: FIRST PERSON, INC. VS. YUEN, PHILIP D/B/A WEMAIL ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Set Aside
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 17, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT PHILIP YUEN's Motion To Set Aside Defendant Philip Yuen's motion to set aside default judgment is granted, and the default and default judgment are vacated as void on their face pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure, § 473(d). Although substitute service on an individual may be proper at the defendant's "usual mailing address," before an individual may be served by substitute service, the burden is on the plaintiff to show that the summons and complaint "cannot with reasonable diligence be personally delivered to the person to be served." Code Civ. Proc. § 415.20(b). Here, the proof of service is facially defective because it does not show that Plaintiff exercised reasonable diligence to serve plaintiff personally at a "proper place" or attempted to locate a proper place. (See American Express Centurion Bank v. Zara (2011) 199 Cal.App.4th 383, 389 ["Two or three attempts to personally serve a defendant at a proper place ordinarily qualifies as "'reasonable diligence.'"]; cf. Hearn v. Howard (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 1193, 1202 [process server attempted to personally serve defendant at the business address on her letterhead and reported by the California State Bar by appearing at that address on three separate occasions]; Ellard v. Conway (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 540, 545-547 [process server attempted to serve defendants at their last known residence, discovered they had moved, and left copy of summons and complaint at their forwarding address, a private post office, and mailed the documents to the same place].)Although it appears that Defendant was aware of the litigation, actual notice is irrelevant on a facial challenge of the judgement and in any event is insufficient alone to confer jurisdiction in the absence of proper service. (See American Express Centurion Bank v. Zara, 199 Cal.App.4th at 392 ["Actual notice of the action alone, however, is not a substitute for proper service and is not sufficient to confer jurisdiction."].) The default and default judgment are vacated. Plaintiff shall return the sum of $25,623.43 previously levied to Defendant within 10 days of the date of this order. Defendant shall provide an address at which he can accept service of summons or authorize his counsel to accept service on his behalf. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: MEDLEY CAPITAL CORPORATION VS. SECURITY NATIONAL GUARANTY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Defendants Motion For Leave To File Cross-Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 17, 2019, Line 2 Defendants Security National Guaranty, Inc. and Edmond Ghandour's motion for leave to file cross-complaint is granted. The court "shall grant" leave to file a cross-complaint "at any time during the course of the action." (Code Civ. Proc. § 426.50; see Silver Organizations Ltd. v. Frank (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 94, 98-99 ["A motion to file a cross-complaint at any time during the course of the action must be granted unless bad faith of the moving party is demonstrated where forfeiture would otherwise result."].) Plaintiff Medley Capital Corporation has not shown that Defendants are acting in bad faith, have unduly delayed in bringing their motion, or that it would be prejudiced by allowing the cross-complaint. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: JAGPREET SUMMAN VS. PIER 39 STUDIO MANHATTAN, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Terminating Or, In The Alternative, Issue And Evidence Sanctions; To Deem Certain Facts Admitted; And For Monetary Sanctions Pursuant To Ccp Sections 2023.010, 2023.020, 2023.030, 2033.280 And Cal. Rule Of Court 2.30 (B) And (D)
rulings.Rulings: DEFENDANT STUDIO SF ACCESSORIES, INC.'s Motion For Terminating Or, In The Alternative, Issue And Evidence Sanctions; To Deem Certain Facts Admitted; And For Monetary Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Stephanie Krmpotic, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's motions is granted. Request for Admissions 1 - 10 shall be deemed admitted. Sanctions are awarded in the amount of $6,745.50, payable within 10 days of entry of order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to JSKrmpotic@LOWBALL.COM with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (JPT/EPS/302)

rulings.Case Title: ZAHIRA SALMA VS. ARRIBA JUNTOS
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice And Motion To Dismiss For Lack Of Subject Matter Jurisdiction Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 17, 2019, Line 10, DEFENDANT ARRIBA JUNTOS Motion To Dismiss For Lack Of Subject Matter Jurisdiction Defendant Arriba Juntos's motion to dismiss for lack of subject matter jurisdiction is denied. First, Defendant waived the protection of the workers' compensation statute by failing to plead it as an affirmative defense in its answer. "Generally speaking, in a lawsuit for personal injury damages, a defendant has the burden of pleading and proving, as an affirmative defense, the existence of the conditions of compensation under the workers' compensation law." (Brown v. Desert Christian Center (2011) 193 Cal.App.4th 733, 739, citing Doney v. Tambouratgis (1979) 23 Cal.3d 91, 96.) There may be exceptions to the rule, such as where the applicability of the rule is raised by the allegations of the complaint or by evidence submitted with a motion for summary judgment. (See Rowland v. County of Sonoma (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 331, 334 [exceptions exist if the complaint directly or indirectly alleges facts indicating an employment relationship or when postpleading motions indicate that the conditions of compensation might exist]; cf. Lucich v. City of Oakland (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 494, 499 [limiting Rowland to its "unique facts" and characterizing its discussion as dicta].) However, none applies here. Second, even assuming that Defendant had not waived the defense and that Plaintiff Zahia Salma was an employee of Arriba Juntos (which she disputes), Defendant has failed to show that Plaintiff's injury was sustained in the course of her employment and arose out of her employment. (Lab. Code § 3600.) Plaintiff was injured as a result of slipping and falling on the stairs of Defendant's building (which was not her workplace) while picking up her paycheck. A later claim for workers' compensation benefits that Defendant apparently submitted on her behalf after this action was filed was denied on the ground that "there is no substantial medical, factual or legal evidence to indicate [she] sustained an injury while employed with Arriba Juntos while insured with Cypress Insurance Company." (Ahumada Decl., Ex. E.) (end of tentative rulings part 1 of 2) =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: ZAHIRA SALMA VS. ARRIBA JUNTOS
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Dismiss For Lack Of Subject Matter Jurisdiction (Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 17, 2019, Line 10, Motion To Dismiss For Lack Of Subject Matter Jurisdiction (Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2) Contrary to Defendant's unexplained contention that it "bears no resemblance to the case at hand," Robbins applies squarely here. Here, as in Robbins, Defendant allowed employees to receive their paychecks either by having them mailed to their home address or by picking them up at any time at its premises; Plaintiff could have easily slipped and fallen even if she did not work for Defendant, just like any other member of the public entering the premises; and here, she did not even work on the premises. Under the circumstances, she did not sustain any injury "arising out of and in the course of the employment" by Defendant, she was not "performing service growing out of and incidental to . . . her employment [or] acting within the course of . . . her employment," and the injury was not "proximately caused by the employment." (Lab. Code § 3600(a)(1),(3).) Defendant's contention that Wright v. Beverly Fabrics, Inc. (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 346 is "more on point to the facts of this case" is mistaken. The employee there was injured while assisting fellow employees in holding up a collapsing shelf, "an activity incidental to her employment in furtherance of her employer's business and protecting its assets." (Id. at 509.) The principle involved was that "[i]njuries sustained while an employee is performing tasks within his or her employment contract but outside normal work hours are within the course of employment," on the rationale that "the employee is still acting in furtherance of the employer's business." (Id. at 508.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.( End of tenative Ruling part 2 of 2) =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: MARIA ELENA MELGOZA ET AL VS. SHREE JALARAM MOTEL, L.P. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 17, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANT SHREE JALARAM MOTEL, L.P., SHREE SHIVA MURUGA, L.P., SHREE JALARAM GENERAL PARTNERSHIP DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Defendants Shree Jalaram Motel, LP, Shree Shiva Muruga LP, Shree Jalaram General Partnership, The Greenwich Inn, Motel Capri, Balwantsinh Thakor, Bhaveshsihn Thakor, Kiransinh Thakor, and Lataben Thakor's demurrer to Plaintiffs' Maria Elena Melgoza and Elder Chocoj's complaint is overruled as to all ten causes of action. Plaintiffs make sufficient allegations to support each cause of action and to lay out their theory for how each of the nine named defendants allegedly played a role in the Labor Code violations asserted in the complaint. (See, e.g., Complaint 1-14, 16-19, 29-30, 40, 51, 54-55, 59-60, 63-64, 68-69, 75, 77, 82, 84, 89, 97-99, 104, 111, 116.) Specifically, pay discrimination based on race is sufficiently alleged at paragraphs 30, 40, and 104 of the Complaint in support of the Equal Pay Act claim. In addition, Plaintiffs are entitled to name defendants under their fictitious business names so long as they amend their complaint to add the true corporate names upon discovery of the proper identities of the defendants. (Code Civ. Proc. § 474 ["When the plaintiff is ignorant of the name of a defendant, he must state that fact in the complaint . . . and such defendant may be designated in any pleading or proceeding by any name, and when his true name is discovered, the pleading or proceeding must be amended accordingly"]; see also Pinkerton's Inc. v. Superior Court (1996) 49 Cal.App.4th 1342, 1349 [describing plaintiffs' actions in suing a defendant under a fictitious business name as "an entirely acceptable procedure"].) Finally, Defendants' demurrer to the prayers for injunctive relief and punitive damages are procedurally improper. A demurrer may only be used to challenge the entirety of a complaint or individual causes of action, not prayers for relief. (Code Civ. Proc. § 430.10; Venice Town Council, Inc. v. City of Los Angeles (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 1547, 1562 ["[A] demurrer tests the sufficiency of the factual allegations of the complaint rather than the relief suggested in the prayer of the complaint."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: ARYA KHOSRAVI VS. BLUE BUS TOURS COMPANY, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 17, 2019, Line 14, DEFENDANT BLUE BUS TOURS COMPANY, LLC DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendant Blue Bus Tours Company, LLC's demurrer to plaintiff Arya Khosravi's first amended complaint is overruled. There are no actions pending between the same parties "on the same cause of action" as those pled in the FAC because plaintiff's first PAGA action and the class action involve only wage and hour claims, while the causes of action asserted in the FAC are for retaliation and wrongful termination. (Code Civ. Proc. § 430.10(c); see Bush v. Superior Court (1992) 10 Cal.App.4th 1374, 1384 [actions were not on the same cause of action where they did not involve invasion of the same primary right].) Even though the claims in all three actions arise out of Plaintiff's employment by Defendant, the central issues in the FAC-whether Plaintiff was subjected to unlawful retaliation and was wrongfully terminated-are not raised in the other two pending actions, and there is no probability of inconsistent rulings or duplication of efforts. Further, although Plaintiff purports to state a cause of action in the FAC under PAGA for penalties "on behalf of himself only," there is no such thing as an "individual" PAGA claim. "A plaintiff asserting a PAGA claim may not bring the claim simply on his or her own behalf but must bring it as a representative action and include 'other current or former employees.'" (Reyes v. Macy's, Inc. (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 1119, 1123; see also Kahn v. Dunn-Edwards Corp. (2018) 19 Cal.App.5th 804, 810 n.1 ["[A] PAGA action is only a representative action. 'Permitting pursuit of only individual penalties appears inconsistent with PAGA's objectives.'"].) Because the claims in the FAC assert different primary rights than those in the first PAGA action and the class action, staying the action under the doctrine of priority jurisdiction is not warranted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)
